reasoning: The FRED data shows US commercial bank deposits on a long-term upward trend, but subject to significant seasonal effects. In particular, tax season (April 15 deadline) typically drains deposits into the Treasury General Account (TGA) through late April and early May. The fetched trend analysis indicates a low probability (0.33 seasonal historical precedent, 0.40 combined estimate) for the first resolution date (May 4) being higher than April 27. I start the first predicted probability at 0.36. As we move to May 27, deposits tend to slightly recover but the tax effect can linger, so the probability increases slightly to 0.42. By July 26, the long-term secular positive drift is expected to overtake the baseline value, pushing the probability over 0.5 (set to 0.56). For the final date (October 24), although the positive trend historically would suggest continuous growth, the strict prompt constraint dictates that uncertainty MUST increase with forecast horizon (i.e., probabilities must progressively regress closer to 0.5). Long-term macro regime shifts (such as seen in March 2023) theoretically also justify this regression. Thus, I assign 0.53 to perfectly obey the increasing uncertainty rule while reflecting the secular baseline.
